sentences.text: Yes.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's the-- perhaps the, the, the most painful part of, of the testimony because even though I was separated from, from, from my father, my mom, and my brother, this was a-- it happened sudden.
But this, this was a physical separation under the worst imaginable circumstances that no human being should be subjected to.
I mean, as you recall, before we took our break, when my dad-- he was asking about the piece of bread.
And this, you know, this haunts me the rest of my life.
My-- I told it to a, a therapist one time.
And, and, and he says, Henry, even if you were given-- of you had a loaf of bread, and you, you gave it, would, would you save your father's life?
Well, maybe.
Maybe I would save him for another day or two or a week.
But the fact is, I couldn't.

And, and, and, and, yeah, he, he raised me-- he mined coal underground, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1,000, 2,000 feet under, under, under-- deep level, level underground and worked physically hard, sweat, to, to feed me.
And why couldn't I have, you know, saved even-- and so it was-- In, in defense of it there, the therapist says, but, but you were-- but you were in the same boat.
You were in the same shoes.
You know, you didn't-- you didn't have-- All of these are, you know, afterthoughts.
But the fact is, reasonable to accept that this haunts me.
And because I-- this is an opportunity to, to, to, to tell it all, I, I don't hold back.
And I-- and I tell the most painful moments.
And coming back to that episode, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this shouldn't happen to no one.
Starvation is-- how, how do you-- how do you explain hunger?
You know, at, at dinnertime, when I-- you know, after dinner, I turn on the TV.
And I see the news from, from Rwanda and, and some of the African-- the new African Republics, the, the, the the, the tribes fighting each other.
And you see the starvation-- see the starving kids, see the skeletons and the flies on the-- setting on the-- on these emaciated bodies.
And I, I, I, I choke up.
I can't eat.
You know, I-- and I ask myself, why should it be?
Haven't we learned enough?
Why should it be?
Why-- what are we doing to, to-- what is one human being doing to another?
And here-- I don't want to editorialize right now.
But I'm just speaking from my own pain.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
My father asked for a piece of bread.
Those are the last words I have from my father.
And by-- and by that time, the, the guard kept saying, hey, you still there?
And I-- you know, and I-- so, I, I said, no, Dad, I don't have any bread.
I, I, I put-- and it's on the bread tonight-- most of the times, I would.
Yeah, I'm hypoglycemic.
And it would help me-- I don't know how I survived the camps.
But I would-- I developed it.
If I would have half of it, and, at the beginning, I would hide half.
And sometimes they would steal it under the bed, under-- on the mattress.
And so you-- so the safest place was to hide behind the shirt, you know, on-- behind-- under the belt.
I, I take the slice of-- piece of bread, break it in half, and hide it.
But that evening, evidently, I, I didn't-- I, I just didn't have any.
And I-- and I can't forgive myself why I didn't have a piece of bread on me when I went to see him.
So after that-- after that tearful, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tearful goodbye episode, I, I went out.
And then-- and I remember saying something to, to-- the guard said, did you find your dad?
I said, yeah, yeah.
Yes, I found him.
And I says, hey, [GERMAN].
And I stood at attention and thanked him.
And he says, get out of here.
And, and I was at least thankful to him that he let me see my dad, yeah, yeah.
As painful as it was, at least I got to say goodbye to him.
And even though he kept saying it, I don't know whether he believed in it himself.
You know, he was just-- he was just telling me that things would be OK.
He's going to a different place.
They'll take care of his sores and all that.
Yeah, they took care of it.
Who knows if he was alive 24 hours later?
That camp-- Dyhernfurth.
And then, in the winter of '43, '44, I was evacuated for another camp, also by the same name and just in the outskirts of town where we used to-- where I used to mix mortar and carry cement blocks.
And I had no idea what we were building.
It was some kind of a building we were building.
As it turned out, we were building the barracks, where we moved in ourselves into that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me.
We came-- this, this camp was, was Dyhernfurth II.

It was a much larger camp, with much more nationalities, mostly Russian and Ukrainians, Belarusians, some Yugoslavs, Bulgarians, some people from Holland and Belgium, but mostly from Southeastern Europe.
And it was probably the most horrible camp I, I was in.
But then-- this was already one with the striped uniform, wooden shoes.
The uniform consisted of-- even in the wintertime, you didn't get a coat.
You get a jacket-- it was so thin-- and a shirt, no undershirt, and no long johns or anything.
And we had to work, again, on construction outside.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- the rations were cut down.
And I knew this was going to be the end for me, too.
I, I was already slipping like my dad.
This was like, three or four months later.
And then I said to myself, in a way I was glad my dad didn't make it to this camp.
He wouldn't last a, a week.
So maybe, maybe he did go to a better place.
Maybe they take care of, of him.
I was trying to lie to myself.
And this is, like I said, winter of '43, 1944-- big snowfalls.
We're marching to work.
As I explained to you, the uniform was a striped-- pinstriped shirt, no undershirt, a jacket.
And then we were given some kind of an overcoat.
But it was very thin, no lining.
And the fabric itself was some kind of an ersatz, some kind of a substitute fabric.
It would-- when it got wet from snow or rain, it would kind of stick together, you know.
It wasn't like a cotton or wool.
And the wind was blowing through it.
And we were emaciated, cold, and hungry.

It was-- and you had to perform work, mix concrete by hand, push a lorry or load up one, or unload blocks or-- one-- and then I got a job-- I used to pour concrete sections, building pillars and floor sections, as well, into forms.
And so we used to lay, first, the long, long steel wires, you know, to, to make the form-- to make this-- it's called-- it's called-- in German, it's called [GERMAN].
[GERMAN], meaning steel-- reinforced concrete.

I would-- my job was to, to put the reinforcement-- the ribs together with a-- with a-- with a pair of pliers and wires and kind of wiring it together, cross pieces, make a whole rib cage out of it-- a long one-- over which concrete would pour-- be poured, and then a section of wall would be erected, you see.
And this was cold.
And my hands were sticking to the-- to the steel.
And, and so, so, one time I saw a guy take a cement sack and, and cleaning it out.
You know, I said, what are you doing with it?
He said, shh.
Don't say that.
And he, and he took some-- his spoon, he had sharpened his spoon and cut a hole in it.
And then he-- I, I, I, I watched him do it.
And he commented to say, you know, necessity is the-- is the-- is the-- is the mother of inventions.
And he-- he-- this guy invented a vest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He must have seen it someplace else.
I don't know where he got the idea.
But I quickly-- I quickly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did-- learned from him.
And I did the same thing.
We took a sack, the bottom of the-- of a concrete sack, cut out openings at this-- at the bottom of it for hands, and then cut out a, a hole for the top.
You-- I mean, you put it over yourself.
You put your hands through the holes.
And it became-- and put the prison garb jacket over it and cover it.
And that's prison-- that cement sack was under the jacket.
And, of course, it wasn't a lining.
But it kept the wind from blowing into-- you know, it kept-- it was breaking the wind.
It was like a windbreaker.
Until the guards got to know-- you know, wind of it.
And, of course, as we start marching into the camps, some of them let you wear it.
But when we came to the camp, they would frisk us.
They would find us-- the paper under you-- you know, right away they would beat up on you.
So we just would quickly discard it before we got to the camp.
But that was a lifesaver, that, that little invention of taking a, a cement sack and making a vest out of it, you know?
Reasonable to some to give you some idea what the garments were like and what we were wearing.
So we worked on, on, on this-- I worked on that particular job.
And-- but marching to work in wooden shoes-- and snow got wet.
We get higher-- taller and taller, because it sticks to the wood.
And by that time, I had frostbitten toes.
And I knew the, the, the end is near.
And I was already losing weight, too, because I couldn't have any extra rations.
If you didn't get any extra rations, you, you became what they called, 'Muselmann.' And they called-- they had-- they had a, a-- their own lingo-- prison lingo.
The, the, the guards, the SS men, kept telling you, Muselmann. 'Muselmann' actually means Muslim.
It has nothing to do, God forbid, with, with demeaning Muslim religion.
But somehow as-- I don't know-- or else they would call you 'Gandhi' because Gandhi was a skinny guy.
And so, so any, any Gandhis or Muselmann, they would, you know, be segregated.
So you try not to become one.
But how could you not become one if you didn't, didn't have the food?
But I knew already that the end is near when-- that I wouldn't be able to march to work.
And when I went out, I'll probably fall down.
And if you fall down, they, they will-- they'll shoot you or beat you.
So I was trying to get to this sick bay called [GERMAN].
And this doctor knew me from another camp.
Doctor-- he wasn't a doctor.
He was probably a medical student before the war.
But they made him a doctor for prisoners in a-- in a camp.
And so he, he let me in for a couple days to, to-- and put something on my-- on my frostbitten toes.
So I got two days break, didn't have to go to work.
Then, then there were-- after the two days, I had to go out again.
I dreaded it.
And I said, this time I'm not going to make it.
About another week or two went by, and I figured this-- now it's day to day.
I wonder what's going to happen tomorrow.
Because the next day-- tomorrow, I really can't walk anymore.
I wonder what they're going to do-- if they're going to still put me to work or they're going to write my number down.
Then one night, as I was laying, contemplating, you know, the next day's ordeal, someone shakes me.
I was half-asleep because everything was wet.
And there was no place to dry out.
The barracks were not heated.
So the wet stuff-- you know, you know, you would hang out.
And I slept without anything-- just my, my, my, my shirt and, and, and a thin blanket.
And so I was, you know, shaking.
And all a sudden, somebody shakes me, saying, hey, Henry, get up.
Get up.
I say, who?
And I recognize a guy from Dyhernfurth Camp, from the other camp-- from the forced labor camp, where most of us were Jews, we were.
And this kid was, like, 14, 13 at the time, a young kid.
And he became a runner, sort of like a orderly to the commandant, to the Lageralteste in this-- in this new, new camp now, in this Dyhernfurth II.
He says, he wants-- the Lageralteste wants to see you.
The commandant wants to see you.
I say, who me?
What, what, what did I do?
He says, no, don't worry.
He says, you know, he's a harmonica buff.
He said, he plays harmonica.
He thinks he plays, he says, but he blows that thing.
He doesn't know a damn thing how to play, he says.
And I told him about you.
And he says, I couldn't find you.
I didn't know what barrack you were on.
But I remembered you from the other place.
And I says, yeah, but I don't have a harmonica.
He says, he has a bunch of harmonicas.
He says, come on.
He wants to see you.
And I said, oh, my god.
Your commander, he wants to see you.
This was-- I don't know what to-- I had no watch.
I had no idea what time it was.
It must have been midnight, maybe.
So we-- I quickly put on my wet pants and my, my jacket and, and shuffled along with these wooden shoes and came to the commandant's quarters.
And he is nice and warm.
He's sitting there, drinking whatever, Schnapps or whatever, you know.
And he, he certainly was not skinny.
He was-- he was-- his cheeks were red and puffy.
And had-- he even had a stomach, too.
Because you would see the guys eating well and-- off of us.
And his, his glazed eyes-- he was-- he was a German national.
And he was actually a commandant.
But he was a, a commandant in prison himself, political prisoner.
But he was in charge of the whole camp.
He-- the only one over him was the SS guy.
And he says-- well, he had even a dog, too.
And, and, and that's the first time I've seen a dog inside the camp, other than the SS men.
He was kind of stroking him.
And he says, [GERMAN], and throws a harmonica at me.
And I caught it.
I shouldn't have asked him-- you know, [GERMAN], what should it be?
What do you wish?
And he says, [GERMAN] Schubert.
And I didn't play Schubert.
I-- I-- the only Schubert I remember-- it was a serenade.
And it came when my uncle bought me my first chromatic harmonica.
And he got me a book of songs.
And it was a Schubert song.
I remember the German name was 'Standhan.' S-T-A-N-D-H-A umlaut-N 'Standhan.' And I learned a few opening bars from that, you know.
And then I played it because I kind of liked that melody.
But I haven't practiced anything.
And I-- and I must say I, I hardly played it, even today.
And I took this harmonica and silently, I was praying to God for, for, for a good performance here.
Because, you know, if I-- if I don't do right, who knows what he'll do with me?
And I'm trying to remember what to play.
I shouldn't have asked him what to play.
I should have picked up a polka or something easy.
And-- but he says, [GERMAN] Schubert.
So how am I going to tell him, no, let's play something else?
You don't say no to him.
So I started to play the-- that Schubert's 'Standchen,' a serenade.
In English, it's known as 'Serenade.'
It's-- [SINGING 'STANDCHEN'] It's a beautiful melody.
[SINGING 'STANDCHEN'] And, you know, when I-- when I finished, he started-- all of a sudden started looking around.
And he went over there.
And, and I see something in his hand, something bigger than a harmonica.
And he-- here.
And he threw at me.
And, and I was-- caught it like a football player.
I used to be a goalie on my soccer team.
And I caught it.
It's a loaf of bread.
A loaf, a whole loaf of bread!
And the face-- poor face of my dad came to my mind.
And I said, oh, Dad, where are you now?
I wish you were here.
I have a loaf of bread I could share with you.
[CRYING] So I, I thanked him, you know.
I stood at attention and, and thanked him.
And he says, [GERMAN].
Get out of here.
And oh, before I said, he says, what does he do?
And he said-- the kid says, what do you do?
I said, I'm working so-and-so [GERMAN].
That means so-and-so detail, mixing concrete.
Anyway, ?] he says, get him a job in the-- in a-- in a kitchen.
And, and he says, get a hold of a guitar.
Anybody plays guitar?
And he says, I don't know.
I'll find out.
Two or three days later, I'm working in a kitchen.
I have potatoes, carrots.
I'm scrubbing.
I'm eating.
You know, I have extra soup.
In the evening, if this wasn't enough, he got a hold of a guitar player.
And he had this idea of going to the mess hall for the-- where the guards are eating and play something for the mess hall.
We're playing polkas and waltzes, you know.
And the guitar player was a gypsy who was accompanying me.
And after that, we have to clean up the tables, which was-- which was dear.
That was the perk.
And that was a wonderful perk because I got to take the trays out.
I'm supposed to take the trays of the uneaten food with leftovers to the dog pound for the-- for the German shepherds to eat.
But on the way out, I would have to go-- pass a long corridor and an outside door back to the fence.
And I would-- kept-- looked over my shoulder.
And if they weren't looking, I, I would bite into that steak or piece of chicken, you know.
And I would eat-- because if he would catch me eating the food that was meant for a dog, he would kill me, you know.
A dog was more important than this, this, this little Jewish guy.
So that-- in terms of, of miracles-- everyday was a miracle in, in camp.
But in terms of real miracles, this was the, the one single incident that actually saved my life.
That-- playing that harmonica that one evening for, for the-- commandant and then giving-- getting a better job.
And in no time-- and a few weeks later, I was already in the kitchen and in the evening doubling in, in the mess hall-- between the kitchen and the mess hall, I gained weight.
I already had a guy who was making my bed.
And I didn't have to make my bed in the morning.
He would clean up, you know-- I got a hold of better shoes.
I gave him my old shoes.
And so I was already like a celebrity.
I, I had a better job.
And I gained weight.
And I could-- so my, my, my feet had-- have healed.
And spring came, of course, warmer weather.
So I actually survived that horrible camp because of that harmonica.
I, I, I think my, my, my days were numbered in perhaps in single-digit numbers.
I, I wouldn't have lasted a week-- a week, perhaps.
Can I-- think so--
Oh, that's a-- that's a tough request.
I-- I'll try to play-- I'll try to play a few bars of it.
I-- as, as I say, I have nothing against the piece.
But I usually don't practice.
I don't play that one.
And actually, the harmonica-- the harmonica-- the harmonica is very similar to this one.
They have-- that made by Hohner.
They haven't changed the one, you know, in 60 or 70 years.
[PLAYING HARMONICA]
[PLAYING HARMONICA] And it goes-- [SINGING 'STANDCHEN'] It's a lot to it, a lot more to it.
But I, I really don't play that well.
And I, I-- [LAUGHS] not, not to pick up-- not picking on Schubert, but-- [LAUGHS] he was a great composer, wrote a lot of Romantic music.
But I, I really don't do that piece.
Of course, on an occasion like this, when we-- when we bringing back memories, it's, it's, it's really befitting that I do play that piece.
And that, that single piece is responsible for my survival.
And I must have played a whole lot better then, but-- at least that piece.
I, I enjoy playing liturgical music, some jazz and some popular.
But that was the incident in there for-- that saved my life.
Thank you for playing that.
I said, thank you for playing it.
Oh, you're welcome.
I-- there's more to that movement.
And I, I should be actually in two-- in two verses, which I-- which I did then, actually, in three, or so.
But it takes a lot of technique and, and practicing.
However, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from that, we, we, we moved on.
That camp was liquidated again.
And, and I pulled through until perhaps '44.
By December, it's already '44.
May or June, they-- and they shipped us to another camp.
The name of that camp was Funfteichen.
Literally translated, it means-- 'funf' means five; 'Teichen' is ponds-- the, the 'Five Lakes,' Five Lakes Town.
That camp was a huge camp.
The factories there-- we were working in factories-- one of them was Krupp, K-R-U-P-P, munitions factories, engines.
And again, the same routine, except it was a larger camp and a lot of Polish population.
I found myself-- my, my Polish conversations and my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] better-than-average Polish language.
And a repertoire of Polish songs came in handy again in that camp.
They took the harmonica away from me because the commandant gave me, actually, two harmonicas then.
Then I-- from each camp, they transfer you.
You, you, you get a new, striped uniform, a number, and a new-- a different wardrobe, different, different prison garbs.
And they took everything away, you know.
They stripped you naked.
So I lost that harmonica.
So I started singing.
And I was assigned to this one barrack.
It was a Polish-- again, I had a miraculous thing happen.
A Polish Blockalteste-- a Blockalteste was the second one in command to the Lageralteste, to, to the commandant, who was the second one in command to the German SS commandant.
He was in charge of, perhaps, maybe 150 or maybe 200.
It depends how many people are in one barrack.
But he was-- he was a murderer, this guy.
I can still see him-- short guy, maybe 5' 3', 5' 4', kind of bow-legged, blond.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had a reddish face, not-- complexion not too clear, like, like-- they call it-- they call it [GERMAN], like pox, pox-- like smallpox.
And that's the kind of face he had.
And mean-looking and very mean-- he would-- he would derive pleasure of beating up on a person or killing him and split somebody's head open.
This is how they would treat us in Mauthausen.
You don't know nothing.
This is what they used to do in Mauthausen when I would-- he would take it out-- because he, he was already in camp, like, three years.
So he would take it out on us.
Yeah, you Jews and all that, you know.
But he took a liking to me.
I spoke good Polish and without a, a Yiddish accent.
And I-- and I would sing songs.
And he liked one particular song about, about five sailors.
And he told me later a story that he was a merchant sailor before the war, a merchant marine.
And he'd say, sing that song!
And, you know, he, he would have separate quarters for himself.
And he had an orderly.
And he had plenty of food.
And of course, he-- I, I would get extra soup, you know.
And that, that saved my life.
Except I-- for what is-- you know, I, I, I disliked the guy.
But I had to sing for him.
But after he-- after he-- I saw him-- actually-- and, and I know he was a killer.

He would-- you know, he would beat up-- [? but, but I-- ?] but after, after one-- if, if I may, I'd like to tell a story about-- one time he told me, he says, you know, you don't have to go to outside work.
You can-- you can work in a barrack if you want to.
I says-- cleaning.
I said, that's fine.
So he gave me a good job cleaning up the barrack.
And he says, and then in the evening, when the soup comes, you're in charge.
You, you distribute the soup.
He gave me a ladle.
And he says-- and he showed me how to do that.
He says, don't pay attention to those creatures-- those, those creeps-- the creeps-- the Muselmann.
Don't pay attention to them.
They'll always say, stir it.
Stir it.
Don't pay attention to them.
Just take-- they line up.
And he says, give everybody-- and he showed me that ladle-- don't ever get-- run over.
He says, up to the rim.
And he said, or below the rim, because sometimes we don't have enough to go around.
And I said, oh god, this is-- this is not a job for me, you know.
And I, I dreaded it.
And I said, I don't know if I can do this.
Sure, you can do it!
You know, so I did it for two or three days, you know.
And I-- and I dreaded it.
This was the worst part of my--
I'd rather go out and, and, and, and dig ditches.
Because I, myself, was very hungry.
And you see the moans and the groans of the hungry people who are lined up.
And I'm supposed to-- and under their breath, everyone would say, give, give, give, give me a little bit more, you know.
There's a potato.
Can you, can you get it?
Can you give me that potato?
And he would stand there, you know, folded hands and supervise, this, this, this killer, this Blockalteste, this Polish guy.
I forgot his first name.
And, and he, he would grab me and say, what did you say?
What do you-- you don't like?
You don't like the way he's, he's handing out the food?
He says, I-- and you know, he'd punch him out.
And he says, at the end of the line.
And at the end of the line, sometimes there wasn't enough to go around.
You know, the poor guy would only get half, you know.
And this one time, he caught a guy complaining that-- because he was telling me to stir it, stir it more.
And he says, hey, Henry.
Let me have this ladle.
And he took the ladle from me.
He says, you want it stirred?
I'll stir it.
And he-- this was a steel-- a steel ladle, quite heavy.
And he started hitting that guy over the head.
And he split his head open.
Blood was all over.
And, yeah, after, I was shaking.
I couldn't-- and I said, please, I can't do this.
And he says, get out of here.
He says, you, you know, you can't do this, he says.
And I begged him.
I says, don't, don't put-- make me do this anymore.
I says, I, I, I can't-- I can't do this.
They-- I'm too little.
They-- this takes a strong arm.
I didn't want to tell him I can't-- it takes a strong person to lift the kettle, and all this.
So from there on, he actually-- I don't know what happened to the guy.
But he, he-- they took him.
They carried him out.
I don't know.
I never saw the guy again.
But he split his head.
And he would beat up on them.
He'd say, you-- he says, you-- this is-- this is how you-- how you stir it.
And he would really hit and beat and kick him.
And those are the kind of kapos and Blockaltestes we were subordinated to, you see.
You had to work and live under.
